coredata.dc:title: Self-feedback autocatalysis in free radical triggered photosynthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yucheng
Yang, Siwei
Ding, Guqiao
Liu, Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Much attention has been focused on the fabrication approaches of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with efficient, time-saving, environmentally friendly and controllable approaches, which is well relevant to further applications. In this paper, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress in photosynthesis of N doped GQDs (N-GQDs). N-GQDs can be obtained via the simple ultraviolet (UV) irradiation for the mixture of urea, citric acid (CA) and H2O2. No by-product was generated during the preparation process and the yield of N-GQDs is 94 wt%. Compared with GQDs obtained via previously reported methods, the highly homogeneous size of these N-GQDs (2.4–3.1 nm) demonstrated the outstanding morphological control capacity of our suggested approach. More importantly, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress is the key factor for the suggested efficient preparation approach. Such self-feedback autocatalysis polymerization progress under ultraviolet irradiation is a rapid and effective method to obtain N-GQDs with good morphology and optical properties. This ultraviolet excitation-induced mechanism can be a universal method for numbers of nitrogen-containing precursors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Autocatalysis
Free radical
Photosynthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of N-GQDs
Suggested mechanism of preparation process of N-GQDs
The universality of the preparation method for N-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of N-GQDs. Schematic diagram showing the preparation process of N-GQDs.

Characterizations of N-GQDs. (a) TEM, (b) Cs-corrected HR-TEM, (c) FFT image, (d) AFM image and (e) Raman spectrum of N-GQDs. Inset of a: size distribution histogram of N-GQDs. (f) MALDI-TOFMS spectrum of N-GQDs. (g) MALDI-TOFMS spectrum results in the m/z range of 2800–3000.

Chemical structures and optical properties of N-GQDs. (a) XPS C 1 s spectra and (b) N 1 s spectra of N-GQDs. (c) Zeta potential curve of N-GQDs. (d) UV−vis absorption (black curve), PLE (blue curve) and PL (red curve) spectra of N-GQDs. The typical band at 210 nm can be due to the π -
π
*
transition of aromatic sp
2 domains, and the band at 280 nm can be due to the n -
π
*
transition between the nitrogen-containing group (C˭N-) and sp
2 domains. (e) The Tauc plot (hν = αhν
r , where α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and frequency of radiation, respectively). The factor r = 2, suggesting that N-GQDs are a direct bandgap material (Eg = 3.60 eV). (f) The excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behavior (excitation wavelength from 315 to 405 nm). Only a slight maximum emission wavelength shift (10 nm) was observed, suggesting N-GQDs’ wavelength independence feather.

Self-feedback autocatalysis progress in the preparation process of N-GQDs. (a) Mechanism of N-GQDs synthesized from urea and citric acid. The P-QDs are formed which can accelerate the polymerization reaction. (b) Reaction time-response curve. The reaction rate is low in the initial period (0–8 min), whereas sustainably grows during the increase period (8–16 min) until the end of the reaction, suggesting the autocatalysis effect of P-QDs. (c) Free radical capture (·OH) and non-nitrogen involved comparative experiments. The lower reaction rate as well as production after adding DPPH (black curve) was observed, indicating hydroxyl free radical was the leading participant during the chemical coupling process. The reaction progress of N-containing precursor was much faster than non-nitrogen involved (green curve) progress (8 min v.s. > 45 min), revealing that lattice N is greatly advantageous with higher polymerization efficiency. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A brief summary for the GQDs yield of reported preparation methods.
Comparison of different N-containing precursors for N-GQDs synthesis.

head.ce:title: Self-feedback autocatalysis in free radical triggered photosynthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology, ShanghaiTech University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Much attention has been focused on the fabrication approaches of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with efficient, time-saving, environmentally friendly and controllable approaches, which is well relevant to further applications. In this paper, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress in photosynthesis of N doped GQDs (N-GQDs). N-GQDs can be obtained via the simple ultraviolet (UV) irradiation for the mixture of urea, citric acid (CA) and H2O2. No by-product was generated during the preparation process and the yield of N-GQDs is 94 wt%. Compared with GQDs obtained via previously reported methods, the highly homogeneous size of these N-GQDs (2.4–3.1 nm) demonstrated the outstanding morphological control capacity of our suggested approach. More importantly, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress is the key factor for the suggested efficient preparation approach. Such self-feedback autocatalysis polymerization progress under ultraviolet irradiation is a rapid and effective method to obtain N-GQDs with good morphology and optical properties. This ultraviolet excitation-induced mechanism can be a universal method for numbers of nitrogen-containing precursors.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered as a remarkable material applied for energy storage, photoelectric conversion, catalysis, and biological industries [1–5]. Chemical doping (such as B, N, S, P etc.) can extend the application of GQDs with the effective bandgap tuning [6]. So far, much attention has been focused on the fabrication approaches of GQDs with efficient, time-saving, environmentally friendly and controllable approaches, which is well relevant to further applications [7–10]. The most common fabrication approaches of GQDs include top-down and bottom-up methods [11,12]. Top-down method implies that raw sp2 carbon material (such as graphite, graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nano tube and C60) is handled through chemical cutting or mechanical exfoliation [7–11]. The decomposition progress of graphite is always operated with oxidants and strong acids/bases under tough conditions (such as high temperature) [13]. Therefore, many surface groups (such as oxygen-containing groups) will be attached and do damage to the hexatomic structure [14,15]. On the other hand, the morphology (lateral size and thickness) of top-down method is hard to control, which makes it a difficult way to achieve precise preparation [11]. The bottom-up method refers to reactions in which the GQDs are fabricated by aromatic compounds or other aromatic structures of molecules. This method always utilizes chemical solution synthesis. The reaction progress can be precisely controlled by choosing appropriate precursors and experimental conditions. The particle size is relatively uniform which benefits to further purification [12]. However, the traditional solvothermal bottom-up methods have the disadvantage of poor crystallinity due to unordered polymerization process. This badly limits the quality of the final products and leads to a low productivity [16–20]. Great efforts have been made to develop efficient, time-saving, environmentally friendly and controllable preparation approaches for GQDs. However, the new approaches are still necessary.

To solve above problem, we have previously developed a new synthesis approach for GQDs utilizing an ultraviolet irradiation operation [21]. In this method, oxygen-containing aromatic compounds were chosen as the precursor. Under ultraviolet irradiation, the polymerization process occurred as a directional reaction, thus forming a well-designed GQDs with good crystallinity. Unfortunately, the unavoidable oxygen-containing groups derived from precursor bring out surface defects. This may lead to a decomposition reaction and heterogeneity of the products, making it still inefficient as well as time-consuming. Herein, we report a free radical triggered photosynthesis of N doped GQDs (N-GQDs). N-GQDs can be obtained via the simple ultraviolet (UV) irradiation for the mixture of urea, citric acid (CA) and H2O2. No by-product was generated during the preparation process and the yield of N-GQDs is 94 wt%. Compared with GQDs obtained via previously reported methods, the highly homogeneous size of these N-GQDs (2.4–3.1 nm) demonstrated the outstanding morphological control capacity of our suggested approach. More importantly, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress is the key factor for the suggested efficient preparation approach.

Urea, CA, H2O2, proline, 4-aminopyridine, o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine and 3, 4-Dihydroxypyridine were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, P. R. China). All chemical solvents and reagents are analytical grade and require no additional purification before use. The water used in all experiments was deionized water.

Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation progress of N-GQDs. Urea and CA were selected as the precursor. Typically, 10 mL, 5 mM urea and 15 mL, 10 mM CA aqueous solution were mixed. Then, 5 mL 20 mM H2O2 aqueous solution was added in above mixed solution. The obtained mixture was further exposed under an ultraviolet (UV) source (wavelength 185–254 nm, 500 W, Guoda Shanghai, China Co., Ltd.) at 25 °C for 20 min. Significant discoloration occurred during the reaction as shown in Fig. 1. No by-product was generated during the preparation process. Thus, above yellow solution was freeze dried directly and the yellow N-GQDs powder was further obtained. The yield of N-GQDs is 94 wt%. Table 1 illustrates the GQDs yield of reported preparation methods, which reveals that our suggested approach has obvious superiority for quick and sensitive response time, which is greatly faster than other methods before.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a shows the transition electron microscopy (TEM) image of the obtained N-GQDs. The products were clearly visible with uniform size. The counting result (200 particles in TEM image, Fig. 2a inset) indicates the highly homogeneous size distribution (2.4–3.1 nm) of these N-GQDs. The full width at half maximum of the Gauss distribution curve is only 0.4 nm. Compared with GQDs obtained via previously reported methods, the highly homogeneous size of these N-GQDs indicates the potential outstanding morphological control capacity of our suggested approach. Detailed morphology of products was observed in the spherical aberration corrected high resolution TEM (Cs-corrected HR-TEM, Fig. 2b). Defect-free with typical hexagonal lattice structure can be observed. Fast Fourier transform (FFT, Fig. 2 c) shows a high crystalline structure and significant nonstandard six-fold symmetry.

Matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOFMS) was carried for the further confirmed the extreme uniform size distribution of these N-GQDs. Contrarily, no signal of oligomers can be observed which indicates the no by-products were generated in the preparation process. Moreover, the products show the main molecular ion peak at m/z = 2400–3200. The corresponding lateral size is 2.4–3.1 nm which agree with the statistical result (Fig. 2f). The refined structure of the MALDI-TOFMS spectrum further demonstrated the accurate control for the morphology of N-GQDs thus formed. It is clear that, six main peaks can be observed in the MALDI-TOFMS spectrum. These peaks located at m/z = 2473.52, 2650.62, 2833.84, 3023.19, 3218.66 and 3420.25 can be attributed to the orthohexagonal N-GQDs with lateral size of 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 2.9, 3.0 and 3.1 nm, respectively. Moreover, the repeating unit of m/z 26, 39 and 52 to the reflection of their edge structures can be observed in the local enlarged MALDI-TOFMS spectrum (Fig. 2f). The repeating unit of m/z = 39 expected to be C3H3 bonded to a zigzag edge of N-GQDs (schematically shown in violet part the inset of Fig. 2g inset). The mass of 26 and 76 are expected to be C2H2 and C6H4 bonded to an armchair edge of N-GQD (schematically shown in blue and red part the inset of Fig. 2g inset).

Tapping-mode AFM imaging (Fig. 2d) shows that the height of the N-GQDs is average 0.4 nm which indicates the monolayer structure of N-GQDs. Furtherly, the Raman spectrum of these N-GQDs was carried out (Fig. 2e). The spectrum shows peaks of a D band at 1315 cm−1, a G band at 1545 cm−1, and a 2D band at 2689 cm−1. The ID/IG ratio (ca. 0.24) is relatively low, indicating the good crystallinity of N-GQDs, which is well corresponded with the above results.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum of N-GQDs ( Fig. 3a-b and S1) shows typical doping C 1s peaks at ca. 284.8 and 288.3 eV, a N 1s peak at ca. 399.1 eV, and an O 1s peak at ca. 532.0 eV. The O and N atomic content was 8.9 at% and 41.6 at%, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3a) can be fitted by three peaks at 284.7, 285.9 and 288.1 eV corresponding to the signals of C‒C/C˭C, C‒N/C˭O, and C˭N/C˭O, respectively. The N 1s XPS spectrum can be fitted at 398.8, 399.9 and 401.0 eV which can be classified as pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphic N species, respectively (Fig. 3b). The relative high proportion of graphic N (ca. 8.7 at%, compared to the total N species) indicates the N atom played a part during polymerization progress under intense excitation light source. The hydrophilic groups (such as pyridine N, pyrrole N, graphic N and C˭O) result in the outstanding water solubility of N-GQDs. Indeed, the Zeta potential of N-GQDs is − 58.2 mV (Fig. 3 c).

Optical properties of N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 3d-f. UV–vis adsorption spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows two bands at around 210 and 280 nm. The typical band at 210 nm can be due to the π -
π
*
transition of aromatic sp
2 domains. The band at 280 can be due to the n -
π
*
transition between the nitrogen-containing group (C˭N-) and sp
2 domains [21]. The optical bandgap (Eg) of N-GQDs is estimated from the Tauc plot (hν = αhνr), where α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and frequency of radiation, respectively. For a direct bandgap material, r = 2. Otherwise r = 1/2 for an indirect bandgap material [31]. Fig. 3 e displays a good linear fit when using r = 2 suggesting that N-GQDs are a direct bandgap material. The Eg value of N-GQDs was thus determined as 3.60 eV by measuring the x-axis intercept of an extrapolated line from the linear portion of the curve.

The maximum PL excitation (λex) and PL emission (λem) wavelengths are 360 and 420 nm, respectively (Fig. 3d). The Stokes shift (ΔνSt) and the full-width half-maximum (FWHM) are 60 and 80 nm, respectively, indicating a low energy loss and a weak self-absorption effect. It's worth noting that, the maximum PL excitation wavelength corresponds to the Eg of N-GQDs. This indicates the PL process of N-GQDs can be attributed to the band gap transition. Indeed, with the band gap transition, the quantum yield (φ) of N-GQDs is 0.72 (using quinine sulphate as a reference) which is higher than previously reported GQDs [32–37]. The excitation-wavelength-dependent PL behavior was also investigated (Fig. 3 f). With increased excitation wavelength from 315 to 405 nm, only a slight maximum emission wavelength shift (10 nm) was observed. It is known that band gaps of GQDs are often size-dependent [5]. The good size uniformity and crystallization of N-GQDs (shown in Fig. 2) can result in a certain band gap, which can be the main role for the excitation-independence phenomenon.

The mechanism of reaction progress forming N-GQDs under ultraviolet irradiation was proposed. Fig. 4a illustrates the schematic diagram of the interactivity of precursor and H2O2 molecules. The H2O2 molecules are excited by photons (wavelength between 185 and 254 nm), generating a large amount of hydroxyl free radicals instantly. Hydroxyl radicals can be captured by precursor rapidly, producing a spot of primary graphene quantum dots (P-QDs) via oxidative polymerization. The P-QDs plays strong catalytic part for the decomposition of H2O2, greatly accelerated the formation of massive hydroxyl free radicals, which participated in further oxidative polymerization progress and significantly improve the production rate of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suggested mechanism of preparation process of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The suggested mechanism was further demonstrated. As shown in Fig. 4b, with the increased reaction time, the concentration of N-GQDs increased nonlinearly. This can be due to the obvious catalytic performance of P-QDs. Furtherly, the radical trapping tests were performed in order to confirm the important role of hydroxyl free radicals during the polymerization progress. As shown in Fig. 4c, the concentration growth rate of N-GQDs shows a continuous increase behavior during the whole reaction. The growth lasted for 8 min. The comparative experiment introduced with the radical scavenger (100 ppm, 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radical 2,2-diphenyl-1-(2,4,6-trinitrophenyl)-hydrazyl, DPPH) shows that the corresponding reaction time was 24 min, which was much slower than reaction without the radical scavenger. Change of the reaction time indicates that hydroxyl free radical was the leading participant during the chemical coupling process. The whole progress followed the typical hydroxyl-induced free radical polymerization characteristics.

Interestingly, the change rate of the N-CQDs’ concentration progressively increased with the whole progress despite an alleviation at the end of reaction. This indicates a typical self-feedback autocatalysis progress in which the as-synthesized P-QDs ulteriorly serve as the catalyst for subsequent polymerization. It is suggested that the lattice N (including pyridine N, pyrrole N and graphic N) within the 2D lattice play an important part during catalytic progress. The Gibbs free energy of catalytic reaction can be reduced due to the interface adsorption of H2O2 on the nitrogen sites [38,39]. The controlled experiment was performed in which the GQDs was chosen as the precursor with no nitrogen involved. The non-nitrogen involved polymerization progress is comparatively time-consuming (45 min v.s. 8 min of non-nitrogen and nitrogen involved progress, respectively). This indicates the lattice N is the key factor in regulation of the interface state, which is greatly advantageous to the improvement of polymerization efficiency. In general, the self-feedback mechanism, in which large amounts of free radicals are catalytically engendered with assist of P-QDs, acts as the main reason of the rapid preparation for N-GQDs in our approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The universality of the preparation method for N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Above self-feedback autocatalysis polymerization progress under ultraviolet irradiation is a rapid and effective method to obtain N-GQDs with good morphology and optical properties. This ultraviolet excitation-induced mechanism can be a universal method for numbers of nitrogen-containing precursors. Table 2 displays preparations using proline, 4-aminopyridine, o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine and 3, 4-dihydroxypyridine as precursors. All the products with different lateral size were proved to be obtained with a rapid synthesis process. The polymerization progress can be ultrafast due to the self-feedback mechanism as above said.

It is known that GQDs can act as a suitable bioimaging material due to low biological toxicity, high stability, and suitable emission wavelength [40]. The ultraviolet excitation-induced mechanism in this work showed much convenience in preparation of GQDs as well as excellent product’s size uniformity. The band gap of GQDs could be adjusted by choosing suitable precursors or controlling the concentration of reactants. More importantly, the yield of our method was high with no by-products during the reaction progress, which is nontoxic and has obvious biological application superiority.

In summary, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress in photosynthesis of N-GQDs. N-GQDs can be obtained via the simple UV irradiation for the mixture of urea, CA and H2O2. No by-product was generated during the preparation process and the yield of N-GQDs is 94 wt%. Compared with GQDs obtained via previously reported methods, the highly homogeneous size of these N-GQDs (2.4–3.1 nm) demonstrated the outstanding morphological control capacity of our suggested approach. More importantly, we demonstrated the self-feedback autocatalysis progress is the key factor for the suggested efficient preparation approach. Such self-feedback autocatalysis polymerization progress under ultraviolet irradiation is a rapid and effective method to obtain N-GQDs with good morphology and optical properties. This ultraviolet excitation-induced mechanism can be a universal method for numbers of nitrogen-containing precursors. This work not only provided a new approach for the N-GQDs’ preparation, but also revealed the photocatalytic mechanism of N-GQDs.

Yucheng Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Siwei Yang: Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Guqiao Ding: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision. Zhi Liu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The National Natural Science Foundation of China (11804353 and 11774368) and Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (18511110600). There are no conflicts of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2020.116643.
